---
title: "Tactics That Matter"
description: "Flanking, morale and routing, the unit triangle, terrain, and disengaging."
---

Numbers aren't everything. Sparta's battles are decided by **where** units are and
**which way they face** --- flanking, morale, and the rock-paper-scissors of unit
types turn an even fight lopsided.

## Flanking

Hitting a unit from the side or rear deals far more damage *and* morale loss than a
frontal clash:

| Attack angle | Multiplier |
| --- | --- |
| Front | ×1.0 |
| Side (flank) | **×1.5** |
| Rear | **×2.0** |

**Maneuver behind the enemy line.** A regiment pinned frontally by a screen while
your cavalry hits its rear will melt --- and start a rout that spreads.

## Morale & routing

Units don't fight to the last soldier. When a unit loses enough soldiers or takes
flank/rear hits, it **routs** --- it flees (shown faded) and stops counting toward the
battle.

- **Routs are contagious.** A nearby ally watching a unit break is more likely to
  break too. One collapse can cascade down a line.
- This makes morale a *weapon*: you can win by breaking the enemy's will before
  you've killed even half their soldiers.

## Rank cycling (trained units)

A **well-trained** melee unit periodically rotates its ranks --- the tired front
line steps back and a fresh rank takes its place, signalled by a whistle. Cycling
**slows fatigue buildup** (by up to half at full training) and lets the unit
**recover morale even while still fighting**. The better trained the unit, the more
often it cycles. Ranged units don't cycle.

Fatigue builds at a realistic pace --- sustained melee wears a unit down over
**minutes**, not seconds, and resting in the rear restores it just as gradually.
So relief is a deliberate decision for a prolonged fight, not a frantic
every-few-seconds chore.

Training therefore pays off in a long grind: a drilled regiment wears down a green
one of equal numbers, because the green unit tires and wavers first.

## Stamina

Every soldier carries a **stamina pool** that drains through combat and refills at rest.
Each blow thrown costs stamina; throwing blows costs more than absorbing them. Rising from the ground after
being knocked down costs a burst extra. Low stamina lowers the soldier's **condition
factor**, which scales both attack output and defence --- a tired soldier hits softer and
is easier to hit.

So a unit that has been grinding at the front for a while is measurably weaker than one
that just arrived fresh. Rank cycling (above) helps by rotating tired soldiers to the
rear to recover; simply pulling a unit out of melee and letting it rest works too.

## Prone / knockdown

A blow that delivers enough force can **knock a soldier off their feet**. A prone
soldier has no active defence --- only their armour absorbs incoming hits --- and must spend
both time (about two seconds) and stamina to get back up.

Visually, prone soldiers stand out:

- **At normal zoom (mark LOD):** a prone soldier's mark is flattened into a wide
  horizontal sliver so it reads clearly as "lying flat" among the standing marks.
- **Zoomed in (figure LOD):** the figure silhouette is rotated 90° to show a person
  lying on their side.

**Engaged lines resist knockdown.** A unit that is actively locked in melee (and not
in Skirmish stance) braces automatically, raising the impulse threshold needed to knock
a soldier flat. A skirmishing or unengaged unit does not brace and is easier to topple.
A deeper file also helps --- more soldiers absorbing the same front-rank pressure means
fewer individual soldiers pushed past the knockdown threshold.

A cluster of prone soldiers in the front rank is a warning sign: your line is tired or
outmatched there, and each fallen body briefly opens a gap in your defence before they
can rise.

## Line width (frontage)

You set how wide each regiment fights --- <kbd>[</kbd> and <kbd>]</kbd> step the
width by a file, or drag a selected unit's flank grips. The same soldiers spread
across a different shape:

- **Wide and shallow** puts more men in the front rank, so more of them are
  swinging at once --- good for overlapping an enemy's flank or covering ground so a
  foe can't slip around your line.
- **Narrow and deep** stacks more ranks behind a thinner front. Fewer fight at any
  moment, but the column has more bodies to feed forward as the front falls --- it
  holds a chokepoint or punches through one spot rather than spreading thin.

A wider line is easier to outlast in depth and quicker to crumble once it bends;
a deeper one trades frontage for staying power. The width holds as casualties thin
the ranks, so a line you set wide stays wide until you change it.

**Deploy a whole battle line at once.** Select several regiments and drag one flank
line (right-click and drag): the line splits among them. By default it splits to **equal
depth**, so every regiment forms up the same number of ranks --- a uniform front where
bigger units just take a wider slice. That keeps your whole line's staying power even, with
no thin spot for the enemy to punch through. Press <kbd>Y</kbd> to switch to **equal width**
when you'd rather every unit cover the same frontage (small units stack deeper). They sort
left-to-right by where they already stand, so nobody crosses the field to reach a slot ---
a fast way to dress a ragged group into one clean front before the enemy closes. Hold Shift
to place them in selection order instead when you want a specific regiment on a specific flank.

## The unit triangle (rock-paper-scissors)

Three roles, each strong against one and weak to another:

::: {.grid}

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-4}
### 🗡️ Infantry
The reliable line. No special bonus --- your anvil to pin the enemy in place while
something else does the killing.
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-4}
### 🐎 Cavalry
**Fast** --- they charge at a real gallop (~8.5 m/s, far quicker than any foot
unit's advance) --- with a **charge bonus** that grows with closing speed.
Devastating into a flank or rear, but blunted when it charges into braced spears.
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-4}
### 🛡️ Spearmen
Anti-cavalry (marked with a spear). They **blunt the cavalry charge**, so use them
to **screen** your line against enemy horse. Their long spears also **out-reach**
shorter weapons, landing the first blows as lines close (see below).
:::

:::

**The loop:** cavalry beats exposed flanks → spearmen beat cavalry → infantry pin so
your cavalry can get *around* to those flanks. Read the enemy's screen before you
commit your horse.

## Weapon reach

Each weapon has an **effective reach** --- how close two units must get before they
trade blows. A longer weapon reaches that contact distance first, so it lands the
opening strikes of a clash while a shorter-weapon enemy is still closing the gap:

- **Spears** reach furthest (~2.4 m) --- spearmen strike first as the lines meet, on
  top of blunting cavalry charges.
- **Swords** (infantry, cavalry) are the mid-length baseline (~1.3--1.5 m).
- **Archers** carry only a short sidearm (~0.6 m); they're meant to shoot, not duel
  in the press.

So a spear line meeting a sword line head-on gets free hits during the approach ---
another reason to lead with spearmen and let the shorter weapons pin and flank. Combat
now resolves **per soldier** by weapon reach: a spearman strikes any enemy within its
longer reach, while a swordsman beyond its own (shorter) reach cannot strike back. The
*sustained* standoff --- a spear line physically holding swordsmen out at weapon length
--- now emerges from **knockback**: a landed blow shoves the struck soldier back, so a
spear line keeps knocking swordsmen out past their own reach while staying in its. The
lines no longer snap apart by a spacing rule; the gap is the live balance of the press
forward against the knockback, so it breathes as the fight ebbs and flows.

You can **see** the reach difference: zoom in on a clash and each front-rank fighter
thrusts or swings a blade whose length tracks its weapon's reach, so a spear line reaches
visibly further toward the enemy than a sword line.

## Movement speed

Units move at **realistic real-world speeds**, measured in metres per second:

- **Foot** advances to contact at a brisk pace --- heavy **spearmen** slowest
  (~2.2 m/s), **infantry** a touch quicker (~2.6 m/s), light **archers** quickest
  of the foot (~3.0 m/s).
- **Cavalry** charge at a **gallop** (~8.5 m/s) --- roughly three times any foot
  unit's pace.

That gap is a lever: cavalry cross the field and reach an exposed flank far sooner
than infantry can re-form to face them, so commit horse decisively and time your
foot to be where the charge lands. (The faster the gallop at the moment of contact,
the bigger the charge bonus --- so a clean run-up hits harder than a standing start.)

## Pace modes

By default, foot units advance on **AUTO** pace: they walk on the approach, jog
when a ranged enemy is firing at them (to spend less time under missiles), and
sprint at full speed once within about 200 px of the target --- so a charge
arrives at full momentum without wearing out the troops on the march.

**Toggle Walk advance (☰ Menu → Walk advance)** to lock units to walk pace
throughout. This is required for formed stances like shield wall or pike phalanx:
those formations hold their shape only at a walk, and an auto-sprint will
break them. Keep Walk advance on whenever you are using a tight formation that
would crumble if the unit broke into a run.

The AUTO sprint-override also matters for charge timing: a unit that enters
sprint range will accelerate through any missile fire it still faces --- gaining
maximum speed at contact rather than jogging through the kill zone. Use that to
your advantage when committing cavalry or an assault infantry charge.

```{=html}
<figure class="demo">
  <video class="demo-video" controls muted loop playsinline preload="metadata"
         poster="media/pace_modes.jpg">
    <source src="media/pace_modes.mp4" type="video/mp4">
    Your browser can't play this clip --- see the
    <a href="https://github.com/Lacaedemon/sparta">repository</a> to run the game.
  </video>
  <figcaption>Pace modes: regiments walk on approach, jog when enemy archers open fire, then sprint at full charge speed within ~200 px of the target &mdash; all three AUTO phases in one advance. Toggle Walk advance (☰ Menu) to lock units to walk pace throughout.</figcaption>
</figure>
```

## Side-stepping the line

A small lateral move order is carried out as a **side-step**: the unit shuffles
sideways while holding its facing, instead of wheeling to face the move and
wheeling back. That matters when you are dressing a line just before contact.
Wheeling a regiment turns it away from the enemy for the length of the turn ---
its front momentarily points sideways, and the ranks lose their dressing while
the soldiers reshuffle. A side-step keeps every soldier looking at the enemy and
keeps the line aligned, so you can nudge a unit a few paces to close a gap or
overlap an enemy flank without ever presenting your own flank in the process.

The cut-off is roughly a unit's own width: shifts wider than that, or moves that
head forward, turn and march normally. So to reposition laterally in good order,
make the adjustment small --- a short step keeps the line intact, a long one
commits the unit to a march.

## Order stances

Each order can carry a *stance*, armed with a hotkey before you click (a coloured
cursor shows which is active; <kbd>Esc</kbd> clears it). Stances turn the same units
into different tools:

- **Hold** (<kbd>H</kbd>) --- the unit stands its ground instead of chasing bait.
  Anchor a line, or keep a screen in place while your cavalry swings wide.
- **Attack flank** (<kbd>F</kbd>) / **Attack rear** (<kbd>R</kbd>) --- the unit
  maneuvers for the side or back rather than the front, cashing in the ×1.5 / ×2
  multipliers above. Pair it with a frontal pin.
- **Skirmish** (<kbd>K</kbd>) --- a ranged unit kites: it backs off when a threat
  closes inside its firing range, trading ground to keep shooting rather than being
  caught in melee.
- **Support** (<kbd>G</kbd>) --- the unit guards a chosen ally, shadowing it and
  intercepting any enemy that closes on it. Good for gluing spearmen to vulnerable
  horse, or a guard onto your archers.

## Group attack distribution

When you have several units selected and right-click an enemy, press <kbd>X</kbd> to
cycle between two attack modes:

- **Focused** (default) --- every selected unit charges the same target.
  Use it to overwhelm a single regiment quickly.
- **Distributed** --- each unit picks a different nearby enemy, sorted by proximity
  to the clicked target. Extra units cycle through the list if you have more units
  than enemies. Use it to hold an entire line rather than piling onto one regiment
  and leaving the rest unengaged.

The flash message in the top-centre of the screen confirms the active mode. The mode
is sticky --- it stays set until you press <kbd>X</kbd> again.

```{=html}
<figure class="demo">
  <video class="demo-video" controls muted loop playsinline preload="metadata"
         poster="media/group_attack.jpg">
    <source src="media/group_attack.mp4" type="video/mp4">
    Your browser can't play this clip --- see the
    <a href="https://github.com/Lacaedemon/sparta">repository</a> to run the game.
  </video>
  <figcaption>Group attack distribution: three regiments in distributed mode each target a different nearby enemy instead of all piling onto one.</figcaption>
</figure>
```

## Terrain

Two types of terrain patch appear on the battle field:

| Patch | Colour | Effect |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Forest** | Dark green | Passable - units can enter, but movement is slowed to **60%** of normal speed |
| **Hill** | Tan | **Impassable** - units route around it via A\* pathfinding |

Units automatically path around impassable terrain when ordered to move. The forest
is a different trade-off: routing *around* it costs distance; routing *through* it
costs time. Generally it is faster to push cavalry or fast-moving infantry through
rather than around a narrow forest.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Forests slow cavalry charges

A cavalry unit that contacts the enemy while still inside a forest hits with a reduced
charge bonus --- it can't build full speed in the trees. Stage your charges from open
ground whenever possible.
:::

## Order-response delay

Units don't react instantly to orders. There is a brief **reaction delay** before they
execute --- about **0.5 s** for infantry and spearmen, **0.3 s** for cavalry. A unit
already in melee is not slowed by the delay.

This models the time for orders to travel down the chain of command. In practice:

- **Commit cavalry early.** If you wait until the last moment to redirect horse, they
  may not reach the flank in time.
- **Feints are risky.** A unit you pull away from melee takes rear hits during the
  disengage *and* takes a moment to start moving --- don't disengage into a second
  threat without enough margin.

## Disengaging --- a risky lever

Right-click the ground (a **move** order) to pull a unit *out* of melee. It's how
you retreat a battered regiment or redeploy cavalry to a fresh flank.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Disengaging exposes your back
While a unit marches away it shows its **rear** to whatever it left --- so that enemy
gets free **rear hits (×2)** until your unit is clear. Pull out at the right moment,
or pull out into a disaster.
:::

Stop the unit and it re-engages anything nearby. Timing the disengage --- late enough
that the enemy is committed, early enough that you survive the exit --- is one of the
game's sharper decisions.

---

*Source: [`README.md`](https://github.com/Lacaedemon/sparta/blob/main/README.md) ·
combat math lives in
[`scripts/Unit.gd`](https://github.com/Lacaedemon/sparta/blob/main/scripts/Unit.gd)
(`_strike()`, `_flank_multiplier()`).*
